title: What MCP Adds on Top
concept: Standardized discovery, reusable servers across applications, and resources/prompts beyond just callable functions.

exercises.prompt: In traditional function calling, where does the actual execution of the function's code take place?

options.text: The LLM directly executes the code inside its own safe sandbox environment.
The LLM returns structured arguments, and the client application executes the code.
The LLM translates the prompt into HTML and renders it on the user's screen.

exercises.explain: In traditional function calling, the LLM receives the schema, decides which tool to call, and returns the parameters, but the actual execution of the code happens on the developer's client-side application.

cards.title: Beyond One-Off Integration
cards.body: Standard function calling lets an AI run actions, but it requires you to write custom code for every single app. What if tools could self-advertise their capabilities to any AI assistant instantly, just like plugging a mouse into a computer?

cards.title: What is MCP?
cards.body: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets LLMs connect to data sources and tools through a unified server. Instead of rewriting tool-calling code for every new application, you build one MCP server that any client can plug into.

cards.title: The USB Port Analogy
cards.body: Think of traditional function calling like a custom built-in kitchen appliance—it only fits one specific counter slot. MCP is like a USB port: once you plug an MCP server in, any USB-compatible device can use it instantly.

cards.title: Write Once, Connect Anywhere
cards.body: Imagine building an MCP server for your company database. Both your terminal-based developer assistant and your web-based customer support chat can connect to this same server to run queries, without changing their underlying code.

cards.title: Not Just for Functions
cards.body: You might think MCP is only for calling code functions. In reality, it also standardizes 'Resources' (like reading a raw file or database schema) and 'Prompts' (pre-designed templates), offering a complete context-sharing layer.

cards.title: Direct vs Standard Connection
cards.body: Compare how tools connect to apps using traditional function calling versus the Model Context Protocol.

left.text: Custom API wrappers written for every individual app and tool.

right.text: Unified server protocol: plug tools into any app instantly.

exercises.prompt: What extra capabilities does MCP offer on top of traditional function calling?

options.text: It only supports Python code execution.
It adds resources, prompt templates, and standardized server discovery.
It bypasses the LLM entirely to speed up response times.
It replaces traditional functions with raw SQL queries.

exercises.explain: MCP goes beyond simple executable functions by also standardizing static data resources (like documents) and pre-packaged prompt templates.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An MCP server built for one chat client can be reused by a completely different AI application without rewriting the server code.
exercises.explain: Once an MCP server is created, it speaks a standard protocol, meaning any application that supports MCP can connect to it and use its tools without modifications.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept that describes how MCP clients automatically find out what tools a server provides:
exercises.sentence: By using standardized discovery, MCP clients can instantly query any new server to see its list of available tools.
exercises.explain: Standardized discovery is the key feature that allows an MCP client to query a server and automatically learn what tools, resources, and templates are available without manual setup.

exercises.prompt: Which of these descriptions sounds like an AI explaining the architectural additions of MCP?

options.text: Function calling is when you write a JSON schema for a tool, send it to OpenAI, and get back the arguments to run locally on your machine.
Traditional function calling requires developers to manually write API endpoints for every single tool they want the language model to use.
MCP introduces a unified standard containing resources, prompts, and a reusable discovery protocol so clients can dynamically inspect server capabilities.

exercises.explain: The third option uses realistic MCP schema terminology ('resources', 'prompts', and 'reusable discovery protocol') which describes the architectural benefits of MCP.

exercises.prompt: How does the Model Context Protocol (MCP) transform how we build AI-agent architectures compared to traditional one-off function calling?

options.text: MCP replaces the LLM's inner neural network weights with a local database.
MCP forces all functions to run inside the LLM provider's cloud servers.
MCP lets apps auto-discover tools, read static text resources, and share servers across different client apps.
MCP restricts tool use to only plain text files while banning database writes.

exercises.explain: MCP standardizes the entire ecosystem: it allows any LLM-powered app to automatically discover new tools, load reusable prompt templates, and read raw data resources from any compliant server without custom API glue.
